message:
feat(repair,bench): add self-healing demo E2E tests, perf regression baselines, and codec Reed-Solomon improvements

Self-healing demo:
- Add crates/ffs-repair/tests/self_healing_demo_e2e.rs with E2E tests
  validating the self-healing demo runs successfully, produces correct
  structured output, achieves zero data loss, completes within 30s, and
  integrates with the evidence ledger
- Add scripts/e2e/ffs_self_healing_demo.sh shell-based E2E test that
  validates demo exit code, structured output lines, zero data loss,
  evidence ledger round-trip, timing, and deterministic seeding

Performance regression testing:
- Add crates/ffs-block/tests/perf_regression_e2e.rs with deterministic
  workloads against in-memory block devices, comparing metrics against
  stored baselines with configurable threshold tolerance
- Add benchmarks/baselines/ directory with latest.json baseline snapshots
- Add benchmarks/thresholds.toml defining per-operation regression limits

Codec improvements:
- Extend Reed-Solomon codec in ffs-repair with additional error correction
  capabilities

Beads tracker:
- Update .beads/issues.jsonl with current issue status

Co-Authored-By: Claude Opus 4.6 <noreply@anthropic.com>

 {"id":"bd-1bx","title":"Implement degradation level display in TUI dashboard","description":"# Implement degradation level display in TUI dashboard

## GOAL
Add degradation level and pressure indicators to the TUI dashboard so operators can monitor system health.

## BACKGROUND
The graceful degradation system (bd-3tz) produces state that must be visible:
- Current degradation level (0-4)
- CPU/memory/IO pressure gauges
- Recent degradation events

## TECHNICAL REQUIREMENTS

### 1. Extended DashboardSnapshot
```rust
pub struct DashboardSnapshot {
    // ... existing fields ...
    
    // Degradation state
    pub degradation_level: DegradationLevel,
    pub cpu_pressure: f64,      // 0.0 - 1.0
    pub memory_pressure: f64,   // 0.0 - 1.0
    pub io_queue_depth: usize,
    pub degradation_events: Vec<DegradationEvent>,
}
```

### 2. New Panel: System Health
```
┌─ System Health ─────────────────┐
│ Degradation: NORMAL (Level 0)   │
│                                 │
│ CPU:    [████████░░] 78%        │
│ Memory: [██████░░░░] 62%        │
│ I/O:    [███░░░░░░░] 34%        │
│                                 │
│ Recent events:                  │
│   12:34:05 Level 0 → 1 (CPU)    │
│   12:35:22 Level 1 → 0          │
└─────────────────────────────────┘
```

### 3. Color Coding
- Level 0 (Normal): Green
- Level 1 (Background paused): Yellow
- Level 2 (Cache reduced): Orange
- Level 3 (Writes throttled): Red
- Level 4 (Read-only): Blinking Red

### 4. Gauge Widgets
```rust
fn render_pressure_gauge(value: f64, width: u16) -> String {
    let filled = (value * width as f64) as u16;
    let bar = "█".repeat(filled as usize) + &"░".repeat((width - filled) as usize);
    format!("[{}] {:>3}%", bar, (value * 100.0) as u8)
}
```

## TESTS
1. Unit: Gauge rendering at various values
2. Unit: Color coding for each level
3. Integration: Dashboard updates on level change
4. Visual: Screenshot test for layout

## ACCEPTANCE CRITERIA
1. Degradation level always visible
2. Pressure gauges update in real-time
3. Event history shows last 10 events
4.  {"id":"bd-1c4y","title":"EPIC: S3-FIFO for ARC Buffer/Cache Eviction (Graveyard Entry 15.1)","description":"# EPIC: S3-FIFO for ARC Buffer/Cache Eviction (Graveyard Entry 15.1)

## PURPOSE
Replace the current ARC (Adaptive Replacement Cache) buffer cache eviction policy with S3-FIFO, a simpler and often superior eviction algorithm. S3-FIFO uses three FIFO queues — small (admission filter), main, and ghost — with posterior-guided adaptive queue splitting.

## BACKGROUND
From the Alien CS Graveyard entry 15.1:
- S3-FIFO (Yang et al., 2023): three-queue FIFO eviction with admission filter
- Often matches or beats ARC on filesystem workloads with simpler implementation
- No per-access metadata update (unlike ARC which adjusts T1/T2 split on every access)
- Ghost queue provides scan resistance without LRU overhead

## DESIGN

### Three Queues
1. Small Queue (S): admission filter, ~10% of total cache
   - New blocks enter here
   - Evicted from S on first access = not promoted (scan filter)
   - Evicted from S with re-access = promoted to Main
2. Main Queue (M): ~90% of total cache
   - Blocks promoted from S
   - FIFO eviction (no reordering on access)
   - Frequency counter: blocks with count > 0 get second chance
3. Ghost Queue (G): metadata-only (no data), tracks recently evicted
   - Stores block IDs of recently evicted blocks
   - Hit in ghost = block is popular, immediate promotion to Main on re-admission

### Posterior-Guided Adaptive Split
- Bayesian posterior over optimal S/M split ratio
- Update based on ghost hit rate: high ghost hits = S too small
- This extends vanilla S3-FIFO with principled adaptation (alien artifact methodology)

### Drop-In Replacement
- Same BlockCache trait interface as current ARC
- get(block_id) -> Option<BlockData>
- insert(block_id, data) -> Option<(block_id, data)> // evicted entry
- Configurability: total_capacity, small_ratio (default 0.1)

## ACCEPTANCE CRITERIA
- [ ] S3-FIFO implements BlockCache trait
- [ ] Drop-in replacement for ARC (feature flag to select)
- [ ] Benchmark: hit rate comparison vs ARC on filesystem trace
- [ ] Ghost queue provides scan resistance
- [ ] Adaptive split improves over fixed 10/90 split
- [ ] Unit tests: basic FIFO eviction, promotion, ghost hits

## Success Criteria
1. All children closed
2. E2E cache test (bd-1c4y.3) passes
3. S3-FIFO resists sequential scan pollution
4. Hit rate improvement over plain ARC on mixed workloads
5.  {"id":"bd-1c4y.1","title":"Implement S3-FIFO eviction for ffs-block ARC cache","description":"# Implement S3-FIFO eviction for ffs-block ARC cache

## GOAL
Implement the S3-FIFO eviction algorithm as a drop-in replacement for the current ARC cache in the ffs-block crate.

## DESIGN

### Data Structures
```rust
struct S3FifoCache<K, V> {
    small: VecDeque<(K, V, u8)>,      // (key, value, access_count)
    main: VecDeque<(K, V, u8)>,       // (key, value, access_count)
    ghost: VecDeque<K>,                // key only, no data
    index: HashMap<K, QueueLocation>, // O(1) lookup
    small_capacity: usize,            // ~10% of total
    main_capacity: usize,             // ~90% of total
    ghost_capacity: usize,            // same as total
}
```

### Operations
- get(key): lookup in index, increment access_count, return data
- insert(key, value):
  1. If key in ghost: insert directly to Main (popular block returning)
  2. Else: insert to Small
  3. If Small full: evict from Small
     - If evicted had access_count > 0: promote to Main
     - Else: move to Ghost, discard data
  4. If Main full: evict from Main
     - If evicted had access_count > 0: re-insert at tail (second chance), decrement count
     - Else: discard

### Integration
- Feature flag: --features s3fifo to select S3-FIFO over ARC
- Same BlockCache trait interface
- Location: ffs-block/src/cache/s3fifo.rs

## ACCEPTANCE CRITERIA
- [ ] Implements BlockCache trait identically to ARC
- [ ] Small queue filters one-hit-wonder scans
- [ ] Main queue provides frequency-based retention
- [ ] Ghost queue enables fast re-admission of popular evicted blocks
- [ ] Feature flag selects S3-FIFO vs ARC at compile time
- [ ] Unit tests: insert, get, eviction order, ghost hit promotion
- [ ] Property tests: cache never exceeds capacity","status":"closed","priority":2,"issue_type":"task","assignee":"IronPeak","created_at":"2026-02-13T09:24:29.966282698Z","created_by":"ubuntu","updated_at":"2026-02-17T22:34:21.745197242Z","closed_at":"2026-02-17T22:34:21.745130367Z","source_repo":".","compaction_level":0,"original_size":0,"labels":["cache","eviction","s3fifo"],"dependencies":[{"issue_id":"bd-1c4y.1","depends_on_id":"bd-1c4y","type":"parent-child","created_at":"2026-02-13T09:24:29.966282698Z","created_by":"ubuntu","metadata":"{}","thread_id":""}],"comments":[{"id":87,"issue_id":"bd-1c4y.1","author":"Dicklesworthstone","text":"SECOND-PASS REVIEW: This bead is missing a ## LOGGING REQUIREMENTS section.  {"id":"bd-1c4y.3","title":"E2E test: S3-FIFO cache correctness and scan resistance","description":"# E2E test: S3-FIFO cache correctness and scan resistance

## GOAL
Prove that the S3-FIFO cache implementation is correct (no data loss, no stale reads) and provides scan resistance (sequential scan does not evict frequently-accessed hot data).

## TEST SCENARIOS

### Scenario 1: Basic Correctness
1. Insert 1000 blocks into cache (capacity 500)
2. Verify: most recently accessed 500 blocks present (modulo eviction policy)
3. Get all present blocks: verify data matches what was inserted
4. No stale data: every get returns current version or None

### Scenario 2: Scan Resistance
1. Pre-warm cache with 100 hot blocks (access each 10 times)
2. Sequential scan: read 1000 cold blocks (one-hit wonders)
3. After scan: verify hot blocks still in cache (not evicted by scan)
4. This is the key S3-FIFO advantage over LRU

### Scenario 3: Ghost Queue Re-Admission
1. Fill cache, causing eviction of popular block B
2. B enters ghost queue (metadata only)
3. Access B again: should be re-admitted directly to Main queue
4. Verify: B is in Main (not Small), will resist next eviction

### Scenario 4: Capacity Invariant
1. Run 100K random insert/get operations
2. After every operation: verify cache size <= capacity
3. Verify: no memory leak (track allocation count)

### Scenario 5: Drop-In ARC Replacement
1. Run identical workload through both S3-FIFO and ARC
2. Verify: same set of operations produces same observable behavior
   (keys present/absent may differ due to policy, but API contract identical)
3. Verify: BlockCache trait fully implemented (no panics, no unimplemented!())

### Scenario 6: Integration with Filesystem Read Path
1. Mount FrankenFS with S3-FIFO cache (feature flag)
2. Read 1000 files, verify all content correct
3. Read same 100 files repeatedly (hot set), then scan 5000 new files
4. Re-read hot 100 files: verify cache hits (scan did not evict them)
5. Check evidence ledger: cache hit/miss rates logged

## ACCEPTANCE CRITERIA
- [ ] No stale reads or data corruption from cache
- [ ] Scan resistance: hot blocks survive sequential scan
- [ ] Ghost queue correctly promotes re-accessed blocks
- [ ] Cache size never exceeds configured capacity
- [ ] Drop-in replacement for ARC (same trait, same API)
- [ ] Integration test passes with real filesystem workload
- [ ] All scenarios deterministic and CI-executable
- [ ] Logging: cache hit/miss ratio, eviction counts, promotion events","notes":"2026-02-18 FoggyRiver: Added new deterministic S3FIFO ArcCache read-path tests in crates/ffs-block/src/lib.rs: s3fifo_arc_cache_read_path_preserves_written_bytes, s3fifo_arc_cache_scan_resistance_on_read_path, s3fifo_arc_cache_ghost_reaccess_promotes_to_main_queue, s3fifo_arc_cache_capacity_invariant_under_random_access.  {"id":"bd-1c4y.4","title":"Add unit tests for S3-FIFO eviction policy","description":"# Add unit tests for S3-FIFO eviction policy

## GOAL
Provide unit test coverage for the S3-FIFO epic (bd-1c4y). Currently has E2E test (bd-1c4y.3) and benchmark (bd-1c4y.2) but NO unit tests for the three-queue eviction logic, scan resistance, or promotion/demotion paths.

## TEST PLAN

### Queue Structure Tests
1. New entry goes to small queue (S)
2. Second access promotes from S to main queue (M)
3. Eviction from S goes to ghost queue (G)
4. Re-access from G promotes to M (scan resistance)
5. Queue size ratios maintained (S=10%, M=90%)

### Eviction Tests
6. Eviction selects from S first (FIFO order)
7. S empty: eviction falls through to M (FIFO order)
8. Ghost queue bounded (does not grow unbounded)
9. Cache at capacity: insert triggers eviction

### Scan Resistance Tests
10. Sequential scan does not pollute M queue
11. Hot entries survive sequential scan
12. Mixed workload: hot set retained, scan set evicted

### Integration with ARC
13. S3-FIFO can replace ARC eviction policy
14. Cache hit/miss metrics correct under S3-FIFO

## TESTS (REQUIRED)
All 14 tests above. Deterministic.

## LOGGING REQUIREMENTS
- Test failures log queue sizes and contents

## ACCEPTANCE CRITERIA
1. All 14 tests pass

 {"id":"bd-3ib.2","title":"Graveyard alien CS: thread-per-core dispatch for FUSE (Graveyard Entry 15.7)","description":"# Graveyard alien CS: thread-per-core dispatch for FUSE (Graveyard Entry 15.7)

## GOAL
Investigate thread-per-core architecture for FUSE dispatch, replacing the shared-nothing thread pool. From alien_cs_graveyard.md entry on thread-per-core architecture.

## BACKGROUND
Thread-per-core (Seastar/Glommio model) eliminates cross-core synchronization for I/O dispatch. FrankenFS FUSE dispatch currently uses fuser's multi-threaded mode with shared state. Thread-per-core would assign each FUSE fd to a specific core with core-local caches and per-core WAL buffers (see bd-2oah).

## GRAVEYARD INTEGRATION
- References: alien_cs_graveyard.md thread-per-core entry
- Failure modes documented in graveyard: work imbalance, cross-core joins, debugging difficulty
- Adoption wedge: benchmark showing per-core dispatch vs shared-state dispatch on 4+ cores

## DELIVERABLES
1. Design doc: thread-per-core dispatch architecture for ffs-fuse
2. Prototype: core-pinned FUSE dispatch with per-core ARC cache partition
3. Benchmark: throughput comparison at 4, 8, 16 cores
4. Decision: adopt or reject based on measured improvement

## TESTS (REQUIRED)
1. Benchmark: 4K random read IOPS, thread-per-core vs current
2. Benchmark: sequential write throughput, thread-per-core vs current
3. Correctness: concurrent operations produce same results as shared-state

## LOGGING REQUIREMENTS
- Core assignment (info): fuse_fd, core_id
- Cross-core request (debug): source_core, target_core, op_type
- Per-core stats (info): core_id, ops_completed, cache_hit_rate

## ACCEPTANCE CRITERIA
1. Design doc with measured tradeoffs
2. Prototype compiles and passes basic tests
3. Benchmark data for go/no-go decision
4. }
```

) -> Result<ChecksumResult> {

    // 3. Compute actual checksums
    // 4. Compare and report
}
```

### 3. Corruption Handling
- Log corruption with block/extent details
- Attempt RaptorQ recovery if symbols available
- Mark uncorrectable blocks in scrub report

### 4. Progress Reporting
```rust
pub struct ScrubProgress {
    pub total_blocks: u64,

 {"id":"bd-6tme","title":"EPIC: Killer Demo — Self-Healing Durability","description":"# EPIC: Killer Demo — Self-Healing Durability

## PURPOSE
Create a compelling, CI-executable demonstration of FrankenFS self-healing: flip random bits in blocks, show automatic repair using RaptorQ symbols, and output a human-readable repair ledger explaining exactly what was reconstructed. This is the "show, don't tell" proof of FrankenFS's core innovation.

## DEMO SCRIPT

### Setup (automated)
1. Create 128MB ext4 image with FrankenFS (RaptorQ enabled, 5% overhead)
2. Write 100 files with known content (photos, text, binaries)
3. Record BLAKE3 checksums for all files
4. Print: "Filesystem created: 100 files, all checksums verified"

### Corruption (visible to audience)
5. Print: "Injecting corruption: flipping random bits in 3% of blocks..."
6. Corrupt 3% of data blocks with random bit flips
7. Print block IDs and bit positions of corrupted blocks
8. Attempt to read files — show which files have corrupted bytes

### Repair (the magic moment)
9. Print: "Triggering self-healing scrub..."
10. Run scrub daemon (or explicit repair command)
11. Show real-time progress: "Scanning block group 1/32... corruption detected at block 1042... repairing with 4 symbols... OK"
12. Print: "Repair complete in 2.3 seconds"

### Verification (proof)
13. Read all 100 files, verify BLAKE3 checksums
14. Print: "All 100 files verified — zero data loss"
15. Print repair ledger: table of every repair action with timing, blocks, symbols used

### Output Format
```
=== FrankenFS Self-Healing Demo ===
Created: 100 files (128MB image, 5% RaptorQ overhead)
Corrupted: 47 blocks across 8 block groups (3% corruption rate)

Repair Results:
  Block Group  Corrupted  Repaired  Symbols Used  Time
  1            3          3         4             12ms
  4            8          8         12            34ms
  7            6          6         8             28ms

 {"id":"bd-7tn4","title":"PoR for cryptographic durability audit (Graveyard Entry 11.11)","description":"# PoR for cryptographic durability audit (Graveyard Entry 11.11)

## GOAL
Implement Proofs of Retrievability (PoR) for cryptographic verification that stored data is intact and retrievable, without reading the entire dataset.

## BACKGROUND
From Alien CS Graveyard 11.11: PoR (Juels-Kaliski 2007, Shacham-Waters 2008) allows a verifier to challenge a storage system with random queries. The storage system responds with proofs that specific blocks exist and are correct. If the proofs verify, the entire dataset is retrievable with high probability.

## DESIGN
- Challenge: verifier sends random block indices + random coefficients
- Response: storage computes linear combination of challenged blocks weighted by coefficients
- Verification: verifier checks response against precomputed authenticators
- Authenticators: computed once at file creation, stored alongside data

## APPLICABILITY
- Future feature: useful for cloud/remote storage verification
- Complementary to RaptorQ: PoR proves data exists, RaptorQ repairs it if it doesn't
- Useful for auditing: periodic PoR challenges prove filesystem integrity without full scan

## ACCEPTANCE CRITERIA
- [ ] PoR authenticator generation at file creation
- [ ] Challenge-response protocol implemented
- [ ] Verification: false positive rate < 2^-128

 {"id":"bd-820","title":"Implement RaptorQ symbol refresh on dirty group flush","description":"# Implement RaptorQ symbol refresh on dirty group flush

## GOAL
Ensure repair symbols are regenerated when block groups are modified, as mandated by spec §0.4: "A block write that never triggers repair symbol refresh for its group is a spec-conformance bug."

## BACKGROUND
Currently repair symbols are generated at image creation. When blocks are modified:
1. Group becomes "stale" (symbols no longer match data)
2. Must regenerate before corruption can be recovered
3. Two modes: lazy (on next scrub) or eager (on fsync)

## TECHNICAL REQUIREMENTS

### 1. Dirty Group Tracking
```rust
pub struct DirtyGroupTracker {
    dirty: BitVec,  // One bit per group
}

impl DirtyGroupTracker {
    pub fn mark_dirty(&mut self, group: GroupNumber);
    pub fn is_dirty(&self, group: GroupNumber) -> bool;
    pub fn clear(&mut self, group: GroupNumber);
    pub fn dirty_groups(&self) -> impl Iterator<Item = GroupNumber>;
}
```

### 2. Refresh Triggers
- **Lazy**: Background scrub regenerates symbols for dirty groups
- **Eager (fsync)**: Regenerate immediately for committed groups
- **Configurable**: MountConfig.repair_refresh_policy

### 3. Refresh Flow
```rust
pub fn refresh_group_symbols(
    group: GroupNumber,
    device: &dyn BlockDevice,
    storage: &mut RepairGroupStorage,
    cx: &Cx,
) -> Result<()> {
    // 1. Read all blocks in group
    // 2. Regenerate RaptorQ symbols
    // 3. Write to repair storage slots
    // 4. Clear dirty flag
}
```

### 4. Budget Integration
- Refresh respects Cx budget
- Can be interrupted and resumed
- Progress tracked per-group

## TESTS
1. Unit: Dirty tracking set/clear
2. Integration: Write marks group dirty
3. Integration: Scrub refreshes dirty group
4. Integration: fsync refreshes if eager mode
5. E2E: Corruption after refresh -> recovery works

## LOGGING
- Mark dirty (trace): group number, cause
- Refresh start (debug): group, block count
- Refresh complete (info): group, duration, symbol count

## ACCEPTANCE CRITERIA
1. Every modified group eventually refreshed
2. Lazy mode: refreshed within scrub interval
3. Eager mode: refreshed on fsync
4.
